content:
{
  "title": "Contrastive Counterfactual Prompting to Mitigate Social Bias in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models often perpetuate or amplify social biases and stereotypes in generated text. Existing prompting methods have limited ability to reliably reduce these effects without harming model utility.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, mitigating social bias is crucial for fairness and ethical AI. Standard prompt engineering or instruction tuning methods often fail to generalize or are not robust to prompt variations, necessitating new approaches that can systematically reduce biases at inference time.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting technique, Contrastive Counterfactual Prompting (CCP), where each input prompt is paired with a minimally altered counterfactual version that swaps out demographic identifiers (e.g., gender or ethnicity). The model is instructed to generate responses such that outputs for both prompts are comparable and stereotype-neutral, using explicit in-context instructions and demonstrations of neutralization. CCP leverages these contrastive pairs during inference to encourage symmetry and reduce bias without retraining the model.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CCP using the StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs datasets, benchmarking against the Self-Debiasing Prompt baseline and standard instruction prompts. Metrics include the bias score, language modeling quality (perplexity or human evaluation), and the relative reduction in stereotype-aligned generations. A key experiment is a blinded, paired human annotation task comparing outputs from CCP and baselines for both bias and fluency."
}
